In terms of Forward P/E, Stellantis is actually valued more richly than Ford Motor Company (F) and General Motors Company (GM). Its net margin, which has suffered for the past quarters, still stands negative on a TTM basis (however, the company is guiding a low single digits operating margin for H2 2025, as shown below).

Where Stellantis shines is once again their positive net debt position. This is unique for an automaker (no major global carmakers enjoy a positive net debt position), due to the CapEx heavy nature of the industry. It is definitely a strong positive for Stellantis. The limited cost of servicing its debt will compound the diminished CapEx need to launch new American trucks and muscle cars. I think this makes it highly likely we will see positive FCF in 2026, together with an increase in margins.

All eyes on Free Cash Flow

Until early 2024, Stellantis enjoyed double-digits, industry-leading margins. I think we are unlikely to see the company returning to those days, as the world is very different now. Those margins were on the back of a strong post-pandemic demand for vehicles, compounded by a chip shortage that gave strong pricing power to carmakers.

Unfortunately the company ended up not capitalizing on that situation, rather focusing on improving short term financial metrics at the expense of losing its competitiveness in North America. The situation was only exacerbated by tariffs imposed by the Trump administration, which are mentioned by Stellantis as a headwind in their latest earnings report.

Today, the real focus for a turnaround should be, in my view, on Cash Flow. The company’s Levered Free Cash Flow has been on a steady decline for the past years, as the chart below shows (quarterly data from Seeking Alpha).

It is pretty obvious that, long term, a company cannot function (let alone represent a compelling investment proposition) with negative cash flow and breaking-even on margins. Luckily for Stellantis, the company had a strong cash position it mostly inherited from the Fiat Chrysler side of the merger. Stellantis used its cash to navigate the last few quarters without having to take on major debt or diluting shareholders (shares count is stable since the birth of STLA).

However, time is running out. While the company still has ~$22 Billion in cash and enjoys a net debt position, as an investor I want to see their new product strategy working out (especially in North America) and FCF and margin improving. Only then will I be able to recommend a Buy.

Risks: about (and beyond) North America

For the year 2024, Stellantis generated 59% of their AOI (Adjusted Operating Income) in North America, 35% in Enlarged Europe and the remaining 6% in the rest of the world (with a negative AOI from Maserati of about half a billion).

It is clear from these figures that Stellantis is very dependent on North America. At the same time, the company has most of its workforce in Europe, where many of its plants are serving a struggling and shrinking European car market, and political forces make it difficult for the company to become leaner (Stellantis is partially owned by both French and Italian governmental entities).

This, in my view, is the main risk of investing in Stellantis today. The company has a tricky geopolitical exposure, compounded by the current US administration and its unconventional trade policy.

The fact the company has minimal exposure to any region outside of North America and Europe is in itself another risk. While that served Stellantis well in the past (its nonexistent presence in China means the company did not suffer from losing market share there like its European peers), this fact could turn from tailwind to headwind in a rapidly changing geopolitical landscape.

Further elements worth considering before investing (or not) in Stellantis concern autonomous driving. Robotaxis are mentioned by Stellantis in their latest earnings report (see below).

Robotaxis could represent an opportunity or a threat for Stellantis. For example, the company could capitalize on its U.S. presence (where regulation and experimentation on robotaxis is advanced) to eventually bring the same technology in Europe as a leader. Conversely, robotaxis could represent a broad industry headwind as they could theoretically drastically reduce demand for consumer vehicles. Readers can decide for themselves what is more likely.

Personally, I see the automaking industry as a very tough business and one where, as an investor, I hardly want to have much exposure to. For Stellantis specifically, I find any bull case based on robotaxis to be stretched, given we are talking about a company with negative FCF and barely breaking even. Stellantis needs to fix the basics before it can dream about the role it wants to play in the future of mobility.

Conclusion

The market has correctly re-priced Stellantis based on industry tailwinds concerning an ease on U.S. emission standards. This is great for investors who had STLA in their portfolio, but in my view it does not represent an interesting entry point. These tailwinds concern the whole auto industry (at least those car makers exposed to the USA). But Stellantis still has a lot to show.

The company says it will launch five new vehicles in America in the next few months. But how those vehicles will sell, what their financials could look like and whether they will be able to compete in the market and bring the company back to profitability, remains to be seen.

In many of my previous articles on Stellantis, I focused on discussing the product portfolio and the strategic plans of this company, especially for what concerns North America. But I cannot do that today, as we are still too early in the turnaround plan of CEO Antonio Filosa. That's why I reiterate my Hold rating for the company.

I will keep covering Stellantis and assess whether a Buy is warranted once the relaunch plan evolves and new vehicles hit the market.

data.title: The Future Of Forum Energy Technologies Looks Much Brighter Than Its Past
data.description:
Forum Energy Technologies (FET) is one of the top-performing stocks in the entire stock market this year. To be sure, it has surged 106% this year and thus it has outperformed the S&P 500 (+16%) by an impressive margin. On the one hand, as the stock has gone up in an almost straight line, its short-term momentum appears positive. On the other hand, after such a spectacular performance, it is only natural that some investors may fear that the stock has become overvalued and risky. In this article, I will analyze why Forum Energy Technologies remains attractive, despite its steep rally.

Business overview

Forum Energy Technologies is a global company that serves the oil, natural gas and renewable energy industries. It designs, manufactures and sells products that increase the safety and efficiency of energy exploration and production. The company was founded in 2005 and is headquartered in Texas.

Forum Energy Technologies has a terrible performance history. To be sure, it has incurred material losses in 8 of the last 9 years. As a result, the stock dramatically underperformed the broad market until last year. To provide a perspective, the stock plunged 96% from the end of 2015 to early 2020, when the coronavirus crisis struck. Even after its extraordinary rally this year, the stock is still trading 87% lower than its level a decade ago. A markedly poor history always raises a red flag and thus investors should perform their due diligence with special attention before buying this stock.

Fortunately, the future looks much brighter than the past for Forum Energy Technologies. In the third quarter, the company saw its revenue decrease 2% sequentially but grew its adjusted EBITDA 13% sequentially, primarily thanks to cost savings. It thus posted adjusted earnings per share of $0.27. It also enjoyed strong demand in the offshore and international markets, and thus its backlog surged 21%, to a 10-year high level. This certainly bodes well for future growth prospects.

Indeed, management stated that it expects the strong backlog, cost reductions and market share gains to provide a tailwind to the performance of the company in 2026. It also provided remarkably strong guidance for this year, expecting free cash flow of $70-$80 million. At the mid-point, this guidance corresponds to free cash flow per share of $6.15. In other words, the stock is currently trading at just 5.2 times its expected free cash flow this year.

Moreover, Forum Energy Technologies has a highly promising 5-year growth plan in place.

Source: Investor Presentation

Thanks to global economic growth, urbanization and fast-growing energy demand from data centers, the company expects the demand in its markets to grow 9% per year on average over the next five years. It thus expects its annual revenues to double to $1.6 billion by 2030.

In addition, management expects free cash flow to approximately triple until 2030, from $75 million this year to $200-$230 million.

Source: Investor Presentation

At the current share count of 12.2 million, the expected free cash flow of Forum Energy Technologies in 2030 corresponds to free cash flow per share of about $17.60. This is more than half of the current stock price of $31.90. It is thus evident that Forum Energy Technologies has great upside potential if it meets its own guidance.

Analysts seem to agree on the promising growth prospects of the stock. They expect the company to switch from a loss per share of -$0.71 this year to earnings per share of $1.23 in 2026 and grow its earnings per share to $3.25 in 2027.

Debt

The oil and gas industry is infamous for its dramatic cyclicality. It is thus crucial for a company to have a strong balance sheet in order to endure the downturns of this industry. Fortunately, Forum Energy Technologies has learned its lesson from its excessive losses in the past, which were partly caused by a high debt load.

The company has reduced its leverage ratio (Net Debt to EBITDA) to 1.3x, which is certainly healthy. In addition, its net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) is standing at $278 million. This amount is 75% of the market capitalization of the stock and hence it is reasonable.

Notably, net interest expense has exceeded operating income in the last 12 months ($20.5 million vs. $14.8 million). This indicates that the company is not in a rock-solid financial position. Nevertheless, if it keeps growing its free cash flow as per its guidance, it will almost certainly be able to service its debt without any liquidity issues.

Valuation

As mentioned above, analysts expect Forum Energy Technologies to achieve earnings per share of $1.23 in 2026 and $3.25 in 2027. This means that the stock is currently trading at 25.9 times its expected earnings in 2026 and 9.8 times its expected earnings in 2027. The latter is much lower than the median forward price-to-earnings ratio of 13.5x of the energy sector.

On the one hand, as we are two years apart from the end of 2027 and given the poor performance record of the company, a somewhat cautious valuation level is warranted. On the other hand, it is important to realize that the company expects to keep growing beyond 2027, until at least 2030, as per its 5-year plan. As a result, it is likely to grow its earnings per share even further in the years 2028-2030, in the absence of a downturn of the energy industry.

It is also remarkable that the stock is exceptionally cheaply valued relative to its expected free cash flow. The stock is currently trading at only 5.2 times its expected free cash flow this year and only 1.8 times its expected free cash flow in 2030. To cut a long story short, Forum Energy Technologies deserves to trade at a discount to its sector due to its poor performance record, but it appears deeply undervalued given its exciting growth potential.

Risks

The first risk factor has already been mentioned; it is the poor performance record of Forum Energy Technologies. Some investors choose to ignore the past performance of a company, as it belongs to history, but a poor record always raises a red flag regarding the risk of poor business execution in the future. On the bright side, Forum Energy Technologies seems to have learned its lesson from its history and appears to have a promising 5-year growth plan in place.

The other risk factor is the deteriorating outlook of the oil market. OPEC stuck to strict production quotas in recent years in order to support the price of oil but it is now in the process of restoring its output aggressively, with a goal to boost it by 2.0 million barrels per day until the end of next year. As a result, the price of oil has declined towards a 4-year low lately.

Due to the ramp-up of production of the members of OPEC, the Energy Information Administration [EIA] has provided a bearish outlook, expecting the average price of oil to decrease 20% next year, from $69 in 2025 to $55. If this occurs, drilling activity is likely to decrease significantly, hence the business of Forum Energy Technologies will probably be affected.

While the base near-term scenario is negative for the global oil industry, investors should keep in mind that Forum Energy Technologies has a promising 5-year plan in place. The oil industry is so cyclical that a downturn within the next 5 years is almost inevitable. However, investors should be prepared to endure such a downturn and remain focused on the 5-year plan of the company.

Of course, this is much easier said than done. The stock price of Forum Energy Technologies is likely to come under pressure whenever the next downturn of the energy sector shows up. Therefore, the stock is suitable only for investors who can endure stock price pressure and remain focused on the long-term prospects of the stock.

Final thoughts

One of the primary investing criteria of legendary investor Warren Buffett is a solid performance record. Indeed, one can draw some conclusions about the future of a company by examining its past performance. The past performance of Forum Energy Technologies has been daunting, as the stock vastly underperformed the broad market in the decade leading to last year. However, the company seems to have turned a page and has provided a bright outlook for the next five years. Based on the potential of the growth plan, the stock appears deeply undervalued, and thus it is likely to highly reward shareholders in the upcoming years.

data.title: 3 REITs That Big Money Might Snap Up Soon
data.description:
Real estate investment trust, or REIT (VNQ), sector valuations have dropped so low that they have recently become the target of various activist investment firms and private equity companies.

They build stakes in the REITs and then push them to sell assets, buy back shares, and, in select cases, even go private via buyouts to unlock value.

Here are some examples of that in recent months:

  • Plymouth Industrial REIT (PLYM) agreed to be acquired at a 50% premium by private equity company Makarora.
  • Sotherly Hotels (SOHO) is going to be acquired at a huge 150% premium by Kemmons Wilson Hospitality Partners and Ascendant Capital Partners.
  • City Office (CIO) announced a merger with MCME Carell at a 20%+ premium.
  • Whitestone REIT (WSR) received yet another offer to be acquired at a 21% premium.
  • National Storage REIT (NSR/OTC:NTSGF) just received an offer from Brookfield (BAM), led by the legendary investor Bruce Flatt, to be acquired at a 26% premium.
  • Centerspace (CSR) surged in value after it announced that it had hired an advisor to explore a sale of the entire company.

My prediction is that we will see many more REITs get bought out or announce strategic alternatives to unlock value over the coming quarters.

They have been in a bear market for nearly 4 years now, with valuations persistently trading at steep discounts to private market values, and management teams are finally losing patience and becoming more receptive to exploring strategic alternatives.

At the same time, activists and private equity firms see an opportunity to squeeze profits from REITs, even as the rest of the market is fairly expensive.

Here are three REITs we own at High Yield Landlord that could profit from this:

Big Yellow Group (BYG/OTCPK:BYLOF)

Big Yellow Group is the leading self-storage REIT in the UK.

I have personally owned it for about 10 years, first buying it following the Brexit crash, and I hope to hold it for another decade to come.

Self-storage has historically been the most rewarding REIT sector in the U.S., earning nearly 20% average annual total returns over the past decades.

They were so rewarding because the concept was growing in popularity, and these REITs could develop new properties, earning large spreads over their cost of capital.

Big Yellow is now replicating this same model in the UK, which is still 20 years behind. The concept is just now growing in popularity, but there is still 10x less storage space per capita in the UK than in the U.S.:

This has already resulted in very attractive returns for Big Yellow shareholders over the past decades, and given that we are still early, we expect this strong performance to continue for a long time to come:

But rumors recently began circulating that Blackstone (BX) might be interested in buying out Big Yellow Group (BYG/BYLOF).

This led the stock to surge by about 20% in just a few days:

I think that the REIT has more to give.

This initial 20% surge was only based on rumors. No official offer has even been made, and yet, investors are ready to speculate enough to send the stock 20% higher.

This suggests to me that if and when they get an official offer, the premium will be large enough to justify even more upside, especially given that its stock remains historically cheap following the recent surge.

At its peak in early 2022, it traded at nearly 40x AFFO, and that's down to just 19x today:

40x was likely excessive.

But 19x is arguably too low when you consider that the REIT has very little leverage with a low 10% LTV, and it owns a lot of undeveloped land that's not yet generating any cash flow.

Big Yellow has historically traded at these high multiples due to these reasons. Low leverage coupled with rapid growth justifies higher valuations.

I think that long-term fair value is at right around 25x, and Blackstone would likely be happy to acquire it in the low-to-mid 20s just to gain control of the self-storage platform to then scale it across Europe.

Big Yellow Group has by far the most recognizable brand in the UK, and this has a lot of value to big players like Blackstone as they look to expand into this new property sector:

So in short, we think that a buyout attempt is likely here and could result in more upside.

But if not, we are happy to keep holding onto Big Yellow for the long run. It is still slightly discounted and offers a path to double-digit annual total returns when you combine its 4% dividend yield and 8% long-term expected average annual growth rate.

Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (REXR)

Rexford Industrial is one of the leading industrial REITs in the U.S.:

It surged recently after news came out that the famous activist investor Elliot Management had taken a substantial stake in the company.

Their investment was so big that they have now become one of the biggest investors in the company:

Elliott is one of the most renowned activists in the world, commonly targeting undervalued companies and then pushing them to unlock value via major M&A deals.

As an example, Elliott was recently involved in the take-private deal of City Office REIT, which is one of the latest REIT buyouts.

Is Rexford now their next target?

On paper, there are a lot of factors that would make Rexford a great target for a buyout.

It trades at a steep discount to private market values, and that's despite owning very desirable assets, having a strong balance sheet, and having a shareholder-friendly management team.

Even then, I don't think that a buyout is the first step here due to one simple reason: Rexford is very big, owning about $20 billion worth of properties.

That's likely too big a deal even for the biggest alternative asset managers like Blackstone.

Therefore, a buyout likely isn't their first intention.

I think that it is likelier that they will try to push Rexford to sell some of their assets at private market values and then reinvest the proceeds in large buybacks to create and unlock value. If that's not enough, they can then push for a potential buyout once the company has become smaller in size.

This is exactly what they have been doing already. So far this year, they have sold $166 million worth of assets and bought back $100 million worth of shares, and they proudly display this on their latest investor deck.

I think that Elliott is seeing this and thinking that they can pressure them to simply double down on this capital allocation plan and push them to sell assets more aggressively to buy back stock.

Private market cap rates for some of its top assets are in the mid-4s. They are so low because these are truly irreplaceable assets in strategic locations that are already fully built out. Moreover, their lease rates are often deeply below market, offering an opportunity for sizable rent bumps in the future.

In comparison, its own stock is trading at a high-5 implied cap rate.

Therefore, selling assets in the private market to buy back stock in the public market would be highly accretive and immediately expand its FFO and NAV per share.

This is what Farmland Partners (FPI) and BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA) have been doing in recent years to create value.

But the difference between Rexford and these smaller REITs is that it has a far stronger balance sheet, which could allow it to be even more aggressive.

Its current loan-to-value is just about 25%, a historic low. It also has limited debt maturities over the coming year.

This means that the REIT could also further accelerate buybacks by taking on some leverage. With its BBB+ rated balance sheet, it could likely secure long-term debt with an interest rate in the high 4s to buy back shares in the high 5s, resulting in immediate accretion. It could then take its time in gradually selling assets to pay off this leverage.

I would bet that this is the plan.

I don't think that Elliott will seek to sell the whole company, at least for now. It appears more likely to me that they will simply push the management to double down on buybacks as a first step.

Their $100 million buybacks year-to-date are a positive sign, but they don't truly move the needle for a company of this scale.

However, if they did 5-10x more buybacks, it would create meaningful value and also likely help the market sentiment of the stock.

This is likely why Elliott got interested.

We also own a position at High Yield Landlord, as our fair value estimate is about 30% higher than the current share price. While we wait for the upside, we earn a 4.2% dividend yield, and we expect the company to grow its cash flow by 6-8% annually over the long run.

Whitestone REIT

I just recently highlighted Whitestone REIT in a separate article that you can read by clicking here, so I will keep this one short.

The REIT has become one of the clearest buyout targets in the REIT sector, attracting several offers over the past year.

The reason why is simple: Retail properties are today undersupplied and enjoy steady growth. WSR happens to own some of the most desirable retail properties in its peer group, and yet, it trades at the lowest valuation relative to its net asset value:

Its stock is trading at a near 9% implied cap rate, but we think that its assets would be worth closer to a 6-6.5% cap rate in the private market, resulting in a steep discount.

So far, the buyout offers have been rejected because they would undervalue the company, and I agree with the management.

The last offer came in at $15.20, which is far below our $17-20 NAV per share estimate. The discount is especially steep when you consider that the company's NAV is likely to grow significantly in the coming years as its same-property NOI keeps on growing and interest rates return to lower levels, potentially resulting in some cap rate compression.

Therefore, it would not surprise me if Whitestone soon received yet another offer at a higher price. I just hope that the management will remain firm and not sell out for a small short-term profit, because the long-term prospects seem very compelling here.

We have gotten to interview their management several times at High Yield Landlord and hold them in high regard.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Mystery Of m
data.description:
The dividend futures-based model we invented to project the potential future trajectories of the S&P 500 (SPX) starts from a very simple observation:

In this relationship, Ap represents the change in the rate of growth of stock prices, and Ad is the change in the rate of growth of dividends per share. The value m is an amplification factor that varies over long periods of time but can be nearly constant for short-to-intermediate periods of time.

Since we first formulated this relationship in April 2009, we've found that short-to-intermediate periods of time can be as long as decades. But eventually, the value of m does change, and whenever it does, it's a big deal because it means the market regime in which stock prices are set has changed.

The following chart tracks how the value of m has changed from January 2014 through the end of November 2025, which covers the period after we first developed the alternative futures chart we use to visualize the dividend-based model's projections. Our initial observations that set the value of m = 5.0, however, go back to March 2010, when dividend futures as we know them today became a reality and made that estimation possible. We should also note that m was almost certainly at that same level for years before that point in time.

So what is m really?

A potential solution to that mystery was advanced by Xavier Gabaix and Ralph S.J. Koijen in their June 2021 working paper - In Search of the Origins of Financial Fluctuations: The Inelastic Market Hypothesis. For us, this paper immediate leapt to the front of the pack for its potential explanatory power of what m represents because of a simple example they developed to explore one of their propositions. Here is a screenshot of the proposition:

Here is their example:

To think through the economics of Proposition 3, we found the following simple, undergraduate-level example useful. Suppose that there are just two funds: the pure bond fund and the representative mixed fund, which always holds 80% in equities (the magnitude suggested by Figure 1). Then, theta = .08, kappa = 0, so that zeta = 1 - zeta = 0.2 and 1/zeta = 5. Then an extra 1% inflow into the stock market increases the total market valuation by 5%.

Or, to put it more simply, a multiplier of 5 for this simple example, which puts it in the right ballpark for our observations.

It certainly is an intriguing possibility, especially if it can explain how the value of m has changed in the period since 19 February 2020, during which the value of m has held at various constant levels for much shorter periods of time.

References

Xavier Gabaix and Ralph S.J. Koijen. In Search of the Origins of Financial Fluctuations: The Inelastic Markets Hypothesis. National Bureau of Economic Research Working Paper 28967. [PDF Document]. June 2021.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Silvercorp Metals: Growth Intensifies Ahead Of Diversification Into Copper
data.description:
A Buy Rating for Silvercorp Metals Inc. (SVM)

Regarding the NYSE-listed shares of Silvercorp Metals Inc., we adhere to the prevailing market opinion, which recommends a Buy, and upgrade our rating from the previous article, which was Hold.

Our Previous Coverage

Our previous “Hold” rating reflected our view that the stock would continue to play an important role, supported by higher silver prices, which would allow operations to generate robust cash flow. Accordingly, Silvercorp's financial position was therefore assessed as being in a good position ahead of long-term growth initiatives in China and Ecuador, as these projects were whispering at it as a necessary condition to finally put wings on. Since our last article, the stock price has risen by 71.03%, achieving a total return of 71.33% (including the dividend yield ("FWD") of 0.32%). This surpasses the S&P 500's change of +8.02% at the time of this writing. The hold rating was justified by the potentially over-inflated market capitalisation, which, given foreseeable macroeconomic burdens such as persistent tariff-related inflation that would dampen demand for zero-yielding silver bullion due to continued high interest rates, could certainly appear risky at the time. This view was due to silver prices near a 14-year high. Investors sought safe havens in the silver market, which was under such intense pressure due to fears of higher tariffs that no silver was available to borrow or buy, as ETFs had snapped it up down to the last ounce. In line with our strategy of being patient and adding on price dips as long as the highlighted headwinds were blowing, NYSE SVM shares did indeed move sideways for a while before a new upward trend began, suggesting a shift in sentiment towards indecisiveness. Stock market sentiment towards companies like Silvercorp, which is trying to establish a foothold with an important production of copper in Ecuador, was somewhat sobering as speculators reduced their long positions in copper and in the commodities sector in general to an eleven-month low. In light of our fears of market uncertainty triggered by Trump's tariff changes, they eventually materialised, weighing on investors' positions, even though precious metals remained a dominant commodity. The value of investors’ positions was predominantly in precious metals. This was because—and this surprised everyone—expectations for an interest rate cut in September had risen sharply, whereas until then it had been firmly assumed that the Federal Reserve intended to maintain its interest rate policy unchanged. Given a surprisingly weak US jobs report that fell short of expectations, Federal Reserve interest rate analysts were anticipating a possible easing of monetary policy by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC). As Ole Hansen, then head of commodity strategy at Saxo, explained, expectations of a rate cut in September therefore got a boost, Seeking Alpha reported. Zero-income silver bars became more attractive than US Treasury bonds.

About Silvercorp Metals Inc.

Silvercorp Metals Inc. published its financial and operating results for the three months ending September 30, 2025 (hereinafter referred to as “Q2 Fiscal 2026”) on November 6, 2025, via PRNewswire in Vancouver, BC, as reported by Seeking Alpha.

In the “About Silvercorp” section of the Q2 Fiscal 2026, the company describes itself as follows (the company states):

Silvercorp is a Canadian mining company producing silver, gold, lead, and zinc with a long history of profitability and growth potential. The Company's strategy is to create shareholder value by 1) focusing on generating free cash flow from long life mines; 2) organic growth through extensive drilling for discovery; 3) ongoing merger and acquisition efforts to unlock value; and 4) long term commitment to responsible mining and ESG.

Silvercorp Metals Inc., hereinafter referred to as “Silvercorp” or “SVM,” operates the following silver equivalent (silver and gold) and base metal mines in China:

  • An 80% interest in the Ying Mine, an active underground mine for silver, gold, lead, and zinc, located 110 km southwest of Luoyang, China. The Ying Mine has an estimated life of ≈ 15.5 years, which may be extended if Silvercorp's exploration activities manage to unlock additional reserves. In the past twelve months up to and including the third quarter of 2025 ("Q2 Fiscal 2026"), 7.39 million ounces of silver equivalent, 58.32 million pounds of lead, and 7.56 million pounds of zinc were mined in the Ying mining district at AISC, net of by-product credits ($/ounce of silver) of $10.88 to $11.35.
  • A 99% interest in the Gaocheng Mine ("GC"), an active underground mine for silver, gold, lead, and zinc, located 14 km west of Yunfu, China. The estimated life of the GC is ≈ 14 years, whose extension depends on the future successes of exploration activities of Silvercorp. In the past twelve months up to and including the third quarter of 2025 ("Q2 Fiscal 2026"), 0.51 million ounces of silver equivalent, 4.99 million pounds of lead, and 14.4 million pounds of zinc were mined in the GC at AISC, net of by-product credits ($/ounce of silver) of $(6.13) to $12.57.

Silvercorp Metals update: Upgraded to “Buy”

SVM is on track to increase production and reduce production costs. Production will be influenced by the reopening of certain temporarily closed mining areas at the Ying MD mine and the end of adverse weather conditions, which will not affect the GC mine (unlike in Q2 Fiscal 2026). Costs will be reduced by the benefits of increased ore recovery thanks to the more efficient and mechanised shrinkage process at the Ying MD mine and improved ore production at the GC mine (projected up 9% compared to Q2 Fiscal 2026) following the stabilisation of weather conditions. With increased and more cost-effective production, the company will be better positioned to further improve its profit margin, given the record-high prices of its key metals, particularly silver. Rising profit margins will not only be reflected in key performance indicators, which are particularly welcome in North American stock exchanges—namely net income and cash flow—but will also enable SVM to strengthen its financial position and support the continuation of its growth projects, some of which will make significant progress in the coming weeks. In Ecuador, Silvercorp will commence significant low-cost copper equivalent production over an extended period at El Domo and anticipates a feasibility study into the exploitation of high-grade deposits at the Condor North project in the future.

Silvercorp Is Building Its Growth Profile on a Solid Balance Sheet

Silvercorp is well-positioned; the company cannot be less profitable than in Q2 Fiscal 2026, when cash flow from operating activities (+70% year-over-year) was $39.2 million and free cash flow was $11.4 million (+1,800% year-over-year). The future is, of course, uncertain, and things can always go differently than planned. Out there, a wide range of market, macroeconomic, and geopolitical factors has free rein to influence the business climate, and we continue to preach about this factor of unpredictability. But the rapidly growing free cash flow speaks for itself: Silvercorp can fulfill its mission—not only for ongoing expenses, but also for investments, which represent a sizeable financial need that the company generates organically with capacity.

A total of $26.7 million has been applied and capitalized for the China operations, as well as the construction of the El Domo mine and the Condor project permitting activities in Ecuador in the Q2 Fiscal 2026, leading to an annual value of $105-110 million, but possibly less, as projects come on stream (see the next section, “Growth Projects”). The amount of cash and cash equivalents, short-term investments, and the equity investment portfolio is appreciating at least four times the expected annual capitalised expenses, as Silvercorp stated in the Q2 Fiscal 2026:

Ended the period with cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments of $382.3 million, an increase of $5.1 million from the previous quarter, and a portfolio of equity investments with a total market value of $180.2 million, an increase of $108.0 million from the previous quarter; and

Not to mention that SVM can use the following financing for El Domo, as the company also stated:

Subsequent to quarter end, drew down the first $43.875 million from Wheaton Precious Metals International Ltd. under the $175.5 million stream financing agreement for the El Domo mine construction.

Debt-to-EBITDA and Capital Adequacy: The Interest Coverage Ratio is 10.35x, compared to a minimum allowable ratio of 1.5-2.0. This ratio is calculated as the $113.8 million Operating Income over the last 12 months divided by the $11 million Interest Expense over the last 12 months. Combined with net liquidity of $267.3 million (rather than net debt), this means Silvercorp doesn't need to seek confidence from the capital markets. This gives the company an advantage over many competitors in raising additional funding to capitalise on growth opportunities.

Growth Projects

Then,

  • Silvercorp also holds a 75% interest in the El Domo–Curipamba project (14.58 km²), located 6 km southeast of Las Naves in Ecuador. According to a 2021 feasibility study, future open-pit production is: Annual production of 21,390 tonnes of copper equivalent (≈ 47 million pounds CuEq) over the 10-year mine life, projected at ≈US$1.26/lb or ≈US$2,777/tonne CuEq LOM AISC. The prospects for El Domo to become a profitable copper production project are good, as the cost per ton is set against analysts' copper price forecasts. On Tuesday, November 25th, Seeking Alpha wrote the following:

UBS analysts said in a recent report that copper prices likely will rise into next year, raising their March 2026 copper price forecast by $750/ton to $11,500/ton, hiking their June and September 2026 targets by $1,000 each to $12.000/ton and $12,500/ton, respectively, and introducing a new December 2026 target of $13,000/ton.

Copper Futures (HG1:COM)'s $5.16/lb or $11,372.74/tonne at the time of this article was up 24.43% year to date.

The project, with a construction capital of approximately US$240.5 million, remains largely feasible within the company's available liquidity, both now and in the future. This accelerates the start of production, although it is currently scheduled for the second half of 2026.

  • SVM holds a 98.7% interest in the Condor North project, located 35 km south of Fruta del Norte, Ecuador. The project comprises a planned gold-silver-lead-zinc open-pit mine (three adjacent open pits—Los Cuyes, Soledad, and Enma) and an underground mine (Camp). The company intends to submit a Preliminary Economic Assessment (PEA) for the underground operation by the end of 2025 and subsequently conduct a feasibility study. The 2025 MRE, totalling 0.48 million ounces of gold, derived from the indicated resources and the inferred resources of 1.73 million ounces of gold, needs to be upgraded to the higher category of measured resources and ultimately to the reserve category. This will require the necessary permits to enable this expansion and upgrade in the long term.
  • Silvercorp holds a 70 percent stake in BYP, which is located about 220 kilometres southwest of Changsha, the capital of China's Hunan province, and is currently advancing the approval process for the construction of a new gold mine there.

Operating Results: Update as of Q2 Fiscal 2026

Except for zinc, the production of all other metals improved, with 1.66 million ounces of silver and 2,085 ounces of gold, resulting in approximately 1.84 million ounces of silver equivalent in Q2 Fiscal 2026, compared to approximately 1.8 million ounces of silver equivalent in Q2 Fiscal 2025.

Production of lead rose by 7.8% year-on-year to 14.23 million pounds, while zinc fell by 2.76% year-on-year to 5.64 million pounds. Based on these production volumes, Silvercorp recorded corresponding changes in sales of metals compared to the previous year, as shown in the screenshot below from its Q2 Fiscal 2026 results.

Due to some operational issues, AISC/oz increased slightly; however, since the triggers were only temporary problems, costs should soon return to a level more in line with previous periods. Metal prices performed as shown in the following screenshot.

Financial Results: Update as of Q2 Fiscal 2026

Thanks to the rise in the price of silver, the financial figures showed the following positive changes compared to the previous year, as Silvercorp's revenues depend on silver to about 70%:

SVM Silvercorp Metals Inc.'s Stock Price

At the time of this writing, shares of Silvercorp Metals Inc., which trades on the NYSE under the symbol “SVM,” were trading at $7.78 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $1.75 billion.

Technical Analysis

The stock price is 42% above its 52-week mid-point range of $5.48 per share and has been trading in a range of $2.87 to $8.09 over the past 52 weeks. The RSI at 66.68x is approaching overbought territory. Nevertheless, the current price action is very telling at the moment and signals that additional significant gains are in the offing. The stock is showing new upward momentum, especially through the 20-day moving average (MA-20), which approached the 100-day moving average (MA-100) sometime in November 2025 but is now moving above the MA-100 cross again. The 200-day moving average (MA-200) remains well below short-term trends, indicating that the stock—driven by the aforementioned factors—is in a solid long-term uptrend.

The Valuation

The enterprise value/EBITDA ratio is widely used by investors when screening among capital-intensive industries such as precious metals and base metals in mining and exploration to search for potentially undervalued stocks. Based on analysts from Seeking Alpha, just to be fairly valued relative to its peers, as Silvercorp's EV/EBITDA (TTM) of 10.79x was a bit higher compared to the sector median of 10.44x, market participants still preferred shares of Silvercorp's SVM stock on the NYSE, and its market capitalization has increased by +138%, while the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) as a benchmark for its listed peers has increased by +116% over the past 12 months.

This is around the past 12 months, but when looking at the next 12-month period, Seeking Alpha analysts also indicate that Silvercorp has an EV/EBITDA (FWD) of 8.68x, which in this case looks better than the sector median of 8.90x for investors looking for as low values ​​as possible for this ratio, logically leading to the assumption that there will be more participants on the demand side for SVM's NYSE shares.

SVM Silvercorp Metals Inc. has adjusted earnings before interest, income taxes, depreciation and amortisation (adjusted “EBITDA”) attributable to equity owners of $38.3 million for the Q2 Fiscal 2026. Adjusted EBITDA was $35 million for Q1 Fiscal 2026. Forecasts for the quarters following Q2 Fiscal 2026 indicate accelerated metal volume growth due to the resolution of temporary issues. This should have a positive impact on costs, as fixed costs are spread over a larger base. However, a very influential factor impacting margins is the anticipated increase in the price of silver (70% of total revenue) and, to a lesser extent, the record jump in the price of gold. Prices for lead and zinc are expected to remain somewhat subdued due to oversupply.

If Silvercorp generated adjusted EBITDA of $73.3 million in the first half of fiscal year 2026, we believe it is realistic to expect higher adjusted EBITDA going forward. We estimate that the company will achieve adjusted EBITDA of at least $193 million in the next twelve months, assuming the growth rate of 9.43% observed from Q1 to Q2 of fiscal year 2026 remains constant over the following twelve months. At the time of writing, Silvercorp's enterprise value is $1.62 billion. SVM stock, therefore, has an adjusted EV/Fwd EBITDA of 8.4x, which is even lower than the Seeking Alpha analysts' estimates, which contributes to our BUY rating.

Risk Section

  • The Sprott Mining Risk Heat Map shows that the Silvercorp mines in China pose an extreme risk to onshore mining and exploration, while Ecuador, far from the current geopolitical risk between the US and Venezuela, with the deployment of US forces and equipment in the Caribbean, represents a more moderate risk.

  • Silver Spot Price (XAGUSD:CUR) was $57.82 per ounce at the time of this writing, representing a 100.20% upside year-to-date. According to Seeking Alpha, this rise was driven by soaring gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) and speculation about potential tariffs on the more affordable precious metal by the Trump administration. However, there is a risk that the gold price increase will slow once the dispute between Trump and other countries over tariffs subsides. Peace in Ukraine and declining expectations of interest rate cuts by the US Federal Reserve could dampen demand for gold as a safe haven and diminish its appeal, as gold—unlike US Treasury bonds—does not pay interest. But the global geopolitical situation remains tense and uncertain. The constant threat of other conflicts, for example, between the US and Venezuela or between China and Taiwan, is once again driving investors to the safe haven of gold. Due to recent interest rate cuts, the probability that the Fed will cut the key interest rate by 25 basis points at its next meeting on December 10, 2025, is 89.1% at the time of writing. Further rate cuts could follow during 2026, which would lift the interest in gold at the expense of US Treasury bonds.
  • An Altman Z-score of 2.66 means that the overall financial situation is approaching the exit from the uncertain grey zone and entering the safe zone (from a value of 3.0), without any risk of insolvency in the next few years.

Conclusion

Shares of Silvercorp Metals Inc., listed on the NYSE, are performing well: Production is running smoothly, and several challenges have been overcome. This should accelerate operational improvements. Although costs have risen slightly, a return to the lower levels of previous quarters is likely. The price of silver, which serves as both a safe haven and a key element for electrification projects, electric vehicles, AI-powered data centres, the transition to a clean world, and even the production of military technologies, is rising sharply and contributing to SVM's profitability. Silvercorp now has greater liquidity and a good financial leverage ratio. This signals that the company enjoys the confidence of lenders and can strengthen its portfolio of future production projects in Ecuador. Production of copper at the El Domo mine is expected to commence in the second part of 2026.

data.title: ISPY: Can Be An Efficient Hedge Against Slower Market Growth
data.description:
Overview

ProShares S&P 500 High Income ETF (ISPY) offers investors a way to get exposure to constituents of the S&P 500 while collecting a higher rate of income. When I previously covered ISPY, I issued a buy rating due to the fund's resilience compared to some notable peers in the sector. However, I wanted to revisit ISPY to specifically discuss the best use case and market environment that will allow the fund to thrive. With the recent explosion of many option ETFs out there, it can be difficult to determine which fund is a good fit within a diverse portfolio of holdings. In the case of ISPY, I believe that the utility is also dependent on an investor's overall objective and age.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that ISPY's share price has declined by about 0.33%. However, this follows a long recovery from the market's lows back in April. The fund's growth potential is capped due to the inclusion of option writing, but ISPY has demonstrated that it can still partially participate in market rallies. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 9.91% over the same time frame. ISPY currently offers investors a starting dividend yield of 8.3% and issues payouts on a monthly basis.

Although the high yield makes this an enticing choice for income investors, I believe that ISPY's use case is best utilized in specific scenarios. For instance, the inclusion of an option writing strategy means that ISPY is likely to underperform through strong bull markets. However, the fund's strategy can also lead to outperformance in a scenario where market growth slows. Additionally, ISPY doesn't have the most consistent payout history like some peers, which may be a problem for investors that rely on the income generated within their portfolio. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that ISPY implements to generate its income.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, ISPY has an inception dating back to December of 2023. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 0.56%, which may be worth it considering the high yield that investors can receive. As the fund's name implies, ISPY is made up of constituents of the S&P 500 Index while implementing an OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategy to amplify the income levels received from holdings. ISPY writes these options against 100% of the notional value of its assets, with expiration dates that are 1 day out. Rather than writing options on its holdings, ISPY writes options directly against the S&P 500.

There are some tradeoffs to this strategy that work in the fund's favor and increase the risk profile. For instance, the OTM strategy means that ISPY can capture a greater level of price growth from the index. OTM options means that ISPY selects a strike price that is above the current market price of the S&P 500, which allows the fund to participate in market rallies. However, the short expiration date means that the fund has a small time window for its strategy to work in its favor. Market sentiment can shift quickly, and this can disrupt the success of its option writing. However, the benefit is that a shorter expiration date can translate to higher premiums received.

Since the fund is made up of constituents of the S&P 500 Index, the breakdown by sector weight looks similar. For instance, ISPY is most heavily allocated to information technology holdings at 34.78% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to financials and consumer discretionary investments, which accounts for 13.54% and 10.53% of assets, respectively. However, the combination of the top two sectors makes up nearly 50% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these will have the greatest impact on ISPY's daily price movement.

Since ISPY exposes investors to the S&P 500 Index, we can see that the fund provides exposure to many of the highest-quality companies in the world. As we can see below, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest individual position at 7.98% of assets. This is followed by exposure to other notable holdings such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Broadcom (AVGO), just to name a few.

Performance Insights and Ideal Scenario

Despite the fund providing direct exposure to some of the fastest-growing companies in the world, the inclusion of an option strategy means that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide favorable price appreciation over time. As we can see from the performance chart below, ISPY's share price has increased by 14.71% since its inception. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 39.33% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Since the fund may underperform more traditional index-tracking ETFs through a bull market, I believe that the inclusion of ISPY is entirely dependent on an investor's overall objective and goal. For instance, a retired investor may not care about achieving the highest total return. Instead, a retired investor may simply want to maintain exposure to the index while prioritizing higher income generation. Due to the limited growth potential of the fund, I believe that the performance of the fund can be summarized in three potential scenarios:

  1. The S&P 500 rises = ISPY is likely to partially participate in the growth but will ultimately underperform.
  2. The S&P 500 Is Flat/Choppy = ISPY has the potential to outperform since volatility is elevated. This can translate to greater option premiums while also participating in the slower price growth.
  3. The S&P 500 Falls = ISPY has the potential to see smaller losses than traditional ETFs due to the income generation. However, ISPY will struggle to recover from sharp declines since the option writing caps upside potential.

While each scenario may not be as important to retired investors, I think the relevance of these scenarios changes based on an investor's age. If you are a younger investor who has a longer runway of time in the market, you may be aligning yourself for underperformance in the future by choosing a higher income potential in the present. Therefore, it may only make sense for younger investors to utilize ISPY as an accompanying position in a diverse portfolio of holdings that may be better aligned to capture market growth.

Since ISPY's strategy aligns the fund for outperformance during periods of choppiness or uncertainty, ISPY can be utilized as a hedge against the market. For instance, the growth of technology and AI stocks continues to be the main driver of growth over the last year. If sentiment within the technology market shifts and investor optimism declines, market indices can experience a strong pullback. In this scenario, ISPY would still suffer from declines, but the inclusion of distributions would likely soften the impact for investors.

For instance, market indices pulled back from their highs earlier this year in response to market uncertainty linked to President Trump's tariffs. Although ISPY also experienced a pullback alongside the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), ISPY's distributions resulted in a smaller total loss at the bottom of the market. Unfortunately, the fund's operating history is limited, so we don't have many instances to pull from.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.2031 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.3%. The most notable drawback to the fund is that the distributions are highly variable over time. This can be attributed to the fact that payouts are determined by the success of the underlying option writing strategy, momentum of the index and its holdings, as well as the implied volatility levels. Therefore, ISPY may not be the best choice for investors that are seeking the most consistent level of income generation over time. Since the payouts change so often, the listed yield may be different based on the time you are reading this.

Despite the inconsistent payouts, I believe that the main appeal of the fund is the fact that most of the distributions tend to be paid out in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest Section19(a) notice indicates that 93% of the distributions paid YTD were funded by return of capital. Unlike distributions funded by net investment income, return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allow taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. Since return of capital distributions aren't classified as income, they aren't taxed as such. Therefore, a position in ISPY can technically be utilized within a tax-advantaged account with very little tax implications.

However, I want to caution that 'tax efficient' doesn't necessarily translate to tax-free. A small portion of the distributions are still funded by net investment income, which is classified as ordinary dividends and has the highest tax burden for investors. So although the tax burden may be small, there can still be some tax consequences that must be considered by investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on ISPY with the caveat that it only makes sense as a defensive hedge against a flat S&P 500. If you are bullish on the S&P 500, some peer funds may present a better choice due to differences in the frequency in which options are written against their assets. Since ISPY writes options equivalent to 100% of the notional value of its holdings, the limited upside growth may be a lot more relevant during periods of strong bull markets. Additionally, the variable distributions may not be ideal, but the fund has historically issued these payouts in a tax-efficient manner.

data.title: Oracle Q2 Earnings Preview: Focus On The Cocktail Of Debt, Cash Flows, And OpenAI
data.description:
Introduction

Oracle Corp (NYSE: (ORCL)) is scheduled to release its Q2 earnings on Wednesday, December 10 after market close. In this article, I explore the key factors that investors need to watch out for in the company’s Q2 report.

A Recap of Q1 Results

ORCL had a decent start to the year although it missed on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q1 revenues came in at $14.93 billion, and while this was up 12.2% y/y, it missed analyst estimates by $117 million. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.47, and while this was up 5.75% y/y, it missed analyst estimates by $0.01. OCI, the company’s cloud infrastructure business, however, saw its revenues jump 54% y/y. Non-GAAP operating margins came in at 42%, which represented a decline of about 1% y/y and about 2% q/q. The company’s capital expenditures reached $27.4 billion, a significant jump from the Q4 figure of $21.2 billion.

As for the outlook, Q2 revenues are expected to grow in the range between 12% and 14% in constant currency. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to come in the range between $1.61 and $1.65, which would represent a growth in the range of 10% to 12%.

Q2 Expectations

According to LSEG Data, analysts expect Q2 revenues to come in, on average, at $16.22 billion, which would represent a y/y growth of 15.4%. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to come in at $1.64, which would represent a y/y growth of 11.6%.

On the revenues front, ORCL doesn’t have the best of records when it comes to beating analyst estimates, according to LSEG Data, with the company beating analyst estimates in just 2 of the last eight quarters. On average, the company has missed analyst estimates by 0.34%. I expect the company to miss out once again in Q2, especially since the analyst estimates project a revenue growth higher than the higher end of the company’s own guidance. Furthermore, given the supply constraints that continue to plague the datacentre buildout, I am not convinced that the company would be able to generate a significant jump in revenues despite posting record RPOs in the previous quarter.

On the EPS front, the company’s record is better but still mixed when it comes to beating analyst estimates in the last eight quarters. More specifically, the company has managed to beat estimates 50% of the time. Having said that, the magnitude of the beat has been significantly higher than the magnitude of the miss, which results in a positive surprise, on average (1.02%).

Despite the mixed track record of beating analyst estimates, the market has, on most occasions, been impressed with ORCL’s report. This is not surprising to me, given that the market tends to be more focused on the performance of the company’s cloud infrastructure business as well as the growth in the company’s remaining performance obligations (RPOs), both of which, in recent quarters, have been impressive. On average, the 7-day price action post the earnings report has been highly positive, as evidenced by an 8.52% gain. ORCL shares have been pummelled in the last one month, declining around 22%. While they have shown signs of a turnaround in recent days, they still remain beaten down. Given the track record of ORCL shares around the earnings release, I would expect there to be a positive price action post the earnings release, irrespective of how the overall numbers look.

Debt, Cash Flow, and OpenAI: The 3 Focus Areas for Investors in Q2

To say ORCL’s shares have been on a roller coaster ride since its Q1 report would be an understatement. Post the Q1 report, shares surged and subsequently reached all-time highs, thanks to a 359% y/y surge in the company’s remaining performance obligations (RPOs) as well as a whopping 1,529% y/y in multi-cloud database revenues. The company, around this time, also sealed a deal with ChatGPT parent OpenAI to sell $300 billion in computing power over roughly five years.

However, since reaching all-time highs of $345.72, the company’s shares have fallen off a cliff after investors questioned whether the company has the necessary capital to actually fulfil the orders. It also didn’t help that the company, which has negative free cash flows due to its surging capex spending, tapped the bond markets and sold $18 billion in investment-grade bonds in September. There were also reports that the company would be forced to raise “as much as $100 billion” in debt to fund its data centre buildouts.

Moreover, worries surrounding the company’s ability to fulfil its debt obligations was also evidenced last month when there was a surge in buying of credit default swaps tied to ORCL. The CDS five-year spreads tied to ORCL reached as high as 105 bps (or 1.05%) last month, compared to around 38 bps for (GOOGL) and (AMZN), and around 34 bps for (MSFT).

When one factors in all these developments in the last few months, the main point that investors need to look for in the company’s Q2 report is clarity surrounding the company’s funding plans, especially with respect to debt obligations. More specifically, they need to focus on any colour related to the final debt figure that the company plans to raise to build out their AI infrastructure, as well as any colour on when they would expect to be cash flow positive again. Both these factors are critical to estimate whether the company has the financial muscle to fulfil the surging demand. Any abstract deadline or estimates from the management, with no clear rationale, would not be positive, in my view, for the stock.

In addition, investors should also watch out for the company’s guidance on margins. The company, during its analyst day in October, laid out some ambitious goals including gross margins ranging between 30% and 40%, annual EPS of $21 and revenues of $225 billion by FY30. While these are well above analyst estimates and are impressive, investors should look out for the margins that the company is generating today and whether there is any decelerating trend.

Finally, management commentary on any updates with respect to their $300 billion deal with OpenAI would also be a very crucial factor. OpenAI itself requires significant funding to fulfil all its obligations and has been inking deals with all the major AI players. Given that the OpenAI deal is a significant one for ORCL and accounts for a large portion of its impressive RPO, investors, in my view, need to watch out for any progress on this front.

Valuation

ORCL, according to Seeking Alpha, currently trades at a forward P/E of 29.5x, significantly below the 35.5x it was trading during the time I wrote my last article on the company. However, it is still trading well above its 5-year average of 21.3x.

Post the Q1 report, I deployed an Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model to forecast ORCL’s FY26 EPS. The model projected an FY26 EPS of $6.16.

The company currently trades at a forward PEG of 1.3, significantly below the sector median of 1.70 and below the company’s own 5-year average of 1.95. The table below shows the projected FY27 EPS based on these different PEG ratios. The forward P/E multiple remains at 29.5x, and the FY26 EPS remains at $6.16 for all three scenarios.

The average projected FY27 EPS comes out to be $7.29. At a forward P/E of 29.5x, the price target for ORCL comes out to be $215, which suggests limited upside from current levels. As such, from a fundamental perspective, I am maintaining my HOLD rating on the stock.

Having said that, as mentioned earlier, ORCL stock has a stellar track record of performing strongly around the earnings release. Based on this track record, heading into the earnings release, investors could take a small position for the purpose of benefiting from a potential post-earnings momentum. Prior to making this move however, investors should keep in mind that such a trade has two risks: (1) The overall AI trade has shown signs of weakness; and (2) ORCL has been under more scrutiny than ever before, especially from a cash flow and debt perspective.

Concluding Thoughts

ORCL has a mixed track record when it comes to beating analyst estimates. However, the market, especially in recent quarters, has given more priority to the company’s RPO as well as the OCI revenue growth. As such, should the company once again deliver strong growth figures on these metrics, I would expect the stock to rally, especially given that the stock is heading into the earnings report beaten down.

For the Q2 print itself, I would look at three key factors: management’s commentary on the company’s plans with respect to raising debt to fund the AI infrastructure buildout, the timeline of when the company plans to be cash flow positive again, and whether any progress has been made with respect to its deal with OpenAI.

From a valuation perspective, I am maintaining my HOLD rating on the stock give the limited upside at current levels. While investors can still make a trade based on the stock’s strong momentum around earnings releases, the risks of increased scrutiny on the company and the relative weakness of the overall AI trade need to be factored in.
